era_context: late Baroque, Central German Protestant musical culture; career centered on church, court, and collegium music in Leipzig and earlier posts.
public_impression: Often seen as the supreme contrapuntist and systematizer of Lutheran sacred music, with encyclopedic command of forms familiar in his milieu.
deeper_dimensions: His surviving output also includes secular festive works, pedagogical collections, and a pragmatic responsiveness to employers and performing forces—creativity within institutional constraints as much as transcendence above them.

personality.notes: Biographical detail is partial; traits here name widely discussed tendencies in received interpretation, not private psychometrics.

aims: clarity of contrapuntal line within harmonic purpose
liturgical and pedagogical utility where relevant
artistic_identity.notes: Framed from the mix of sacred office, teaching, and civic music-making visible in his career pattern.

characteristic_elements: motoric rhythmic drive in many instrumental genres
chorale-based structures in sacred vocal works
musical_style.notes: Stylistic labels are broad and era-typical; they summarize common textbook characterizations rather than claiming exhaustive uniqueness.

habits: revision and repurposing of material across contexts
working within established genres (cantata cycle, suite, fugue) as creative frame
creative_process.notes: Concrete daily habits are not fully documented; this section encodes plausible working patterns consistent with the surviving work corpus.

source_notes: Dates and posts are standard biographical anchors. Personality and process entries mix consensus career facts with interpretive synthesis; do not treat as undisputed historical claims about inner life.
